1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note the turn to the furnishing of God's house — the lamps and the table — after the laws of priests and feasts. The witnesses note that Gill sets it after the laws of the priests' purity and the people's feasts, God speaking now of things concerning both. The witnesses note that Henry sees here orders for the decent furnishing of the candlestick and table in God's house. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a new word — the LORD speaking again to Moses; the after the feasts — the laws of priests and feasts now given; the the candlestick and table — orders for the furnishing of God's house; the the law repeated — given before, now put in execution.

The puritan expositor (Henry) notes the law for the lamps was given before (Exod. 27:20-21) and is here repeated, probably because it now began to be put in execution when other things were settled — the worship of God ordered down to its furniture.

So the witnesses make the verse the furnishing of the house: The witnesses note the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The witnesses note the after the feasts: the laws of priests and feasts now given. The witnesses note the the candlestick and table: orders for the furnishing of God's house. The witnesses note the the law repeated: given before, now put in execution. The expositors observe the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The expositors observe the after the feasts: the laws of priests and feasts now given. The expositors observe the the candlestick and table: orders for the furnishing of God's house. The expositors observe the the law repeated: given before, now put in execution. The believer is taught to mark the a new word, the after the feasts, the the candlestick and table, the the law repeated, and to honour the LORD in his worship. The verse thus records that God ordered the very furniture of his house — the lamps and the table set in place as the worship of Israel was settled.
2:
"Command the children of Israel, that they bring unto thee pure oil olive beaten for the light, to cause the lamps to burn continually." The witnesses note the people charged to bring pure beaten olive oil for the light, that the lamps might burn continually. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses, the chief magistrate under God, had authority to command Israel to bring what the Lord required. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set vv.2-4 together — the pure oil for the light, the lamps to burn continually before the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the people bring it — israel to provide the oil for the worship; the pure oil olive beaten — the best and clearest oil required; the for the light — to feed the lamps of the sanctuary; the burn continually — a flame never to go out.

The witnesses agree the people themselves provided the oil for the perpetual light: the worship of God was sustained by the offerings of all Israel, and the pure beaten oil, the best and clearest, was a fit emblem of the pure and steady light by which the church shines — the oil of the Spirit feeding a flame that never goes out.

So the witnesses make the verse the oil for the light: The witnesses note the the people bring it: Israel to provide the oil for the worship. The witnesses note the pure oil olive beaten: the best and clearest oil required. The witnesses note the for the light: to feed the lamps of the sanctuary. The witnesses note the burn continually: a flame never to go out. The expositors observe the the people bring it: Israel to provide the oil for the worship. The expositors observe the pure oil olive beaten: the best and clearest oil required. The expositors observe the for the light: to feed the lamps of the sanctuary. The expositors observe the burn continually: a flame never to go out. The believer is taught to mark the the people bring it, the pure oil olive beaten, the for the light, the burn continually, and to honour the LORD in his worship. The verse thus records that the people brought pure beaten oil for the perpetual light — the worship of God sustained by all Israel, the pure oil a fit emblem of the Spirit feeding an unfailing flame.
3:
"Without the vail of the testimony, in the tabernacle of the congregation, shall Aaron order it from the evening unto the morning before the LORD continually: it shall be a statute for ever in your generations." The witnesses note the place and the keeper of the light — outside the veil, ordered by Aaron from evening to morning, a perpetual statute. The witnesses note that Gill sets it on the outside of the veil that divided the holy from the most holy, before the ark of the testimony. The witnesses note that Wesley notes Aaron was to order it either by himself or by his sons (Exod. 25:37). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the without the veil — in the holy place before the most holy; the Aaron orders it — tended by the priest, by himself or his sons; the evening to morning — the light burning through the night; the a statute for ever — a perpetual ministry in their generations.

The witnesses agree the light burned all night in the holy place, just outside the veil where God dwelt: the priest tended it from evening to morning, that the house of God might never be in darkness — a perpetual ministry foreshadowing the unceasing light Christ gives his church, and the watchful service that keeps it burning.

So the witnesses make the verse the keeping of the light: The witnesses note the without the veil: in the holy place before the most holy. The witnesses note the Aaron orders it: tended by the priest, by himself or his sons. The witnesses note the evening to morning: the light burning through the night. The witnesses note the a statute for ever: a perpetual ministry in their generations. The expositors observe the without the veil: in the holy place before the most holy. The expositors observe the Aaron orders it: tended by the priest, by himself or his sons. The expositors observe the evening to morning: the light burning through the night. The expositors observe the a statute for ever: a perpetual ministry in their generations. The believer is taught to mark the without the veil, the Aaron orders it, the evening to morning, the a statute for ever, and to honour the LORD in his worship. The verse thus records that the light burned all night before the veil, tended by the priest — a perpetual ministry foreshadowing the unceasing light Christ gives his church.
4:
"He shall order the lamps upon the pure candlestick before the LORD continually." The witnesses note the lamps set in order upon the pure candlestick, kept continually before the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill, with Jarchi, gives two reasons for "pure" — the candlestick was of pure gold, and was to be kept pure and clean, free from ashes, by the priest. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the second-temple sense, that the priest arranged the lamps after purifying the candlestick and removing the cinders. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the lamps ordered — set in their places by the priest; the pure in substance — the candlestick of pure gold; the pure in keeping — cleansed of ashes and cinders daily; the before the LORD — the light kept clear continually.

The witnesses agree the purity of the candlestick was twofold — pure in substance and pure in keeping: the gold spotless, and the priest daily cleansing it of ash, that the light shine clear; so the church, golden by grace, must be daily purged of what would dim her shining, the lamps trimmed for a steady flame before God.

So the witnesses make the verse the pure candlestick: The witnesses note the the lamps ordered: set in their places by the priest. The witnesses note the pure in substance: the candlestick of pure gold. The witnesses note the pure in keeping: cleansed of ashes and cinders daily. The witnesses note the before the LORD: the light kept clear continually. The expositors observe the the lamps ordered: set in their places by the priest. The expositors observe the pure in substance: the candlestick of pure gold. The expositors observe the pure in keeping: cleansed of ashes and cinders daily. The expositors observe the before the LORD: the light kept clear continually. The believer is taught to mark the the lamps ordered, the pure in substance, the pure in keeping, the before the LORD, and to honour the LORD in his worship. The verse thus records that the lamps were ordered on the pure candlestick — pure in gold and in keeping, the church golden by grace yet daily purged that her light shine clear.
5:
"And thou shalt take fine flour, and bake twelve cakes thereof: two tenth deals shall be in one cake." The witnesses note the twelve cakes of fine flour baked for the table — the shewbread, the bread of the Presence. The witnesses note that Gill notes the fine flour, of wheat, and the finest of it. The witnesses note that Ellicott details the second-temple making — wheat brought as a meat offering, beaten, ground, and passed through twelve sieves each finer than the last. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the fine flour — the finest of the wheat for the bread; the twelve cakes — answering to the twelve tribes of Israel; the two tenth deals each — the appointed measure of every cake; the the bread of Presence — the people set before God on his table.

The witnesses agree the twelve cakes answered to the twelve tribes of Israel: the whole people were represented before God on his table, set continually in his presence — a figure both of Israel held in remembrance before the LORD and of Christ the true bread, who presents his people before the Father.

So the witnesses make the verse the twelve cakes: The witnesses note the fine flour: the finest of the wheat for the bread. The witnesses note the twelve cakes: answering to the twelve tribes of Israel. The witnesses note the two tenth deals each: the appointed measure of every cake. The witnesses note the the bread of Presence: the people set before God on his table. The expositors observe the fine flour: the finest of the wheat for the bread. The expositors observe the twelve cakes: answering to the twelve tribes of Israel. The expositors observe the two tenth deals each: the appointed measure of every cake. The expositors observe the the bread of Presence: the people set before God on his table. The believer is taught to mark the fine flour, the twelve cakes, the two tenth deals each, the the bread of Presence, and to honour the LORD in his worship. The verse thus records that the twelve cakes of fine flour answered to the twelve tribes — the whole people represented before God, a figure of Christ who presents his own before the Father.
6:
"And thou shalt set them in two rows, six on a row, upon the pure table before the LORD." The witnesses note the cakes arranged in two rows of six upon the pure table before the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes the twelve cakes set in two rows. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set vv.6-9 together — the two rows, the frankincense, the weekly renewal, and the eating by the priests. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the two rows — the cakes set in two piles upon the table; the six on a row — the appointed and fixed arrangement; the the pure table — the holy table standing before the LORD; the a holy presentation — the bread set in order, not a common meal; the a continual memorial — the twelve tribes kept perpetually before God.

The witnesses agree the ordered rows on the pure table marked the bread as a holy presentation, not a common meal: set in fixed order before the LORD, the bread of the twelve tribes stood as a continual offering and memorial, the people kept perpetually in remembrance before the God who fed them and dwelt among them.

So the witnesses make the verse the ordered table: The witnesses note the two rows: the cakes set in two piles upon the table. The witnesses note the six on a row: the appointed and fixed arrangement. The witnesses note the the pure table: the holy table standing before the LORD. The witnesses note the a holy presentation: the bread set in order, not a common meal. The witnesses note the a continual memorial: the twelve tribes kept perpetually before God. The expositors observe the two rows: the cakes set in two piles upon the table. The expositors observe the six on a row: the appointed and fixed arrangement. The expositors observe the the pure table: the holy table standing before the LORD. The expositors observe the a holy presentation: the bread set in order, not a common meal. The expositors observe the a continual memorial: the twelve tribes kept perpetually before God. The believer is taught to mark the two rows, the six on a row, the the pure table, the a holy presentation, the a continual memorial, and to honour the LORD in his worship. The verse thus records that the cakes were set in ordered rows on the pure table — a holy presentation, the people kept perpetually in remembrance before the God who dwelt among them.
7:
"And thou shalt put pure frankincense upon each row, that it may be on the bread for a memorial, even an offering made by fire unto the LORD." The witnesses note the pure frankincense set by each row, that the bread might be a memorial and an offering made by fire. The witnesses note that Gill notes the two cups of frankincense, a handful in each, set by each row of cakes. The witnesses note that Ellicott describes the placing of the bowls in the vacant space on the table beside the two piles. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the pure frankincense — set by each row of cakes; the on the bread — that it might be a memorial; the an offering by fire — the frankincense burned unto the LORD; the the acceptance — the people's presentation ascending as a sweet savour.

The witnesses agree the frankincense turned the bread into an offering: when it was burned, the bread that had stood before God all week was accepted as an offering made by fire, a memorial of the people; so the presentation of Israel before God ascended at last as a sweet savour, the incense a figure of the acceptance the people found through what was offered.

So the witnesses make the verse the frankincense memorial: The witnesses note the pure frankincense: set by each row of cakes. The witnesses note the on the bread: that it might be a memorial. The witnesses note the an offering by fire: the frankincense burned unto the LORD. The witnesses note the the acceptance: the people's presentation ascending as a sweet savour. The expositors observe the pure frankincense: set by each row of cakes. The expositors observe the on the bread: that it might be a memorial. The expositors observe the an offering by fire: the frankincense burned unto the LORD. The expositors observe the the acceptance: the people's presentation ascending as a sweet savour. The believer is taught to mark the pure frankincense, the on the bread, the an offering by fire, the the acceptance, and to honour the LORD in his worship. The verse thus records that the frankincense made the bread a memorial offering by fire — the people's presentation ascending as a sweet savour, a figure of their acceptance before God.
8:
"Every sabbath he shall set it in order before the LORD continually, being taken from the children of Israel by an everlasting covenant." The witnesses note the bread renewed every sabbath, set in fresh order before the LORD, by an everlasting covenant. The witnesses note that Gill describes the elaborate manner of the renewal — priests bringing in the new rows and frankincense as others bore out the old. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch mark it taken from the children of Israel by an everlasting covenant. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the every sabbath — the bread set in fresh order each week; the the manner — priests bringing in the new as others bore out the old; the continually — the table never empty before the LORD; the an everlasting covenant — the perpetual presentation of Israel.

The witnesses agree the weekly renewal kept the bread ever fresh before God: as one sabbath's bread was removed, another took its place, so that the table was never empty — a continual covenant memorial, Israel's perpetual presentation before the LORD, never interrupted, never grown stale, the fellowship of God and his people kept always new.

So the witnesses make the verse the weekly renewal: The witnesses note the every sabbath: the bread set in fresh order each week. The witnesses note the the manner: priests bringing in the new as others bore out the old. The witnesses note the continually: the table never empty before the LORD. The witnesses note the an everlasting covenant: the perpetual presentation of Israel. The expositors observe the every sabbath: the bread set in fresh order each week. The expositors observe the the manner: priests bringing in the new as others bore out the old. The expositors observe the continually: the table never empty before the LORD. The expositors observe the an everlasting covenant: the perpetual presentation of Israel. The believer is taught to mark the every sabbath, the the manner, the continually, the an everlasting covenant, and to honour the LORD in his worship. The verse thus records that the bread was renewed every sabbath, the table never empty — a continual covenant memorial, the fellowship of God and his people kept always fresh.
9:
"And it shall be Aaron's and his sons'; and they shall eat it in the holy place: for it is most holy unto him of the offerings of the LORD made by fire by a perpetual statute." The witnesses note the old bread given to Aaron and his sons, to be eaten in the holy place, for it was most holy. The witnesses note that Gill notes the twelve old cakes were divided between the courses of priests, the high priest having half. The witnesses note that Ellicott records the second-temple division — six to the high priest, six among the officiating priests. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Aaron's and his sons' — the old bread given to the priests; the eaten in the holy place — not carried out, for its holiness; the most holy — of the offerings of the LORD made by fire; the the table feeding the servants — what is presented to God shared with his ministers.

The witnesses agree the bread that had stood before God was not common but most holy, and so reserved for the priests to eat in the holy place: what was presented to God was shared with his ministers, the table of the LORD feeding those who served him — a figure our Lord himself recalled when David ate the shewbread (Matt. 12:4).

So the witnesses make the verse the priests' portion: The witnesses note the Aaron's and his sons': the old bread given to the priests. The witnesses note the eaten in the holy place: not carried out, for its holiness. The witnesses note the most holy: of the offerings of the LORD made by fire. The witnesses note the the table feeding the servants: what is presented to God shared with his ministers. The expositors observe the Aaron's and his sons': the old bread given to the priests. The expositors observe the eaten in the holy place: not carried out, for its holiness. The expositors observe the most holy: of the offerings of the LORD made by fire. The expositors observe the the table feeding the servants: what is presented to God shared with his ministers. The believer is taught to mark the Aaron's and his sons', the eaten in the holy place, the most holy, the the table feeding the servants, and to honour the LORD in his worship. The verse thus records that the bread presented to God was most holy, given to the priests to eat in the holy place — the table of the LORD feeding those who served him (Matt. 12:4).
10:
"And the son of an Israelitish woman, whose father was an Egyptian, went out among the children of Israel: and this son of the Israelitish woman and a man of Israel strove together in the camp;" The witnesses note the occasion of the law that follows — a quarrel in the camp involving the son of an Israelite woman and an Egyptian father. The witnesses note that Gill notes his name and his mother's are afterward given. The witnesses note that Henry observes "evil manners beget good laws," and traces the man's mixed parentage — an Egyptian father, an Israelite mother of the tribe of Dan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the mixed parentage — an Egyptian father, an Israelite mother of Dan; the went out among Israel — dwelling among the people; the strove together — a quarrel breaking out in the camp; the evil manners, good laws — the sin occasioning the law that follows.

The puritan expositor (Henry) draws the note of his parentage: neither he nor his father is named, only his Israelite mother; the Jews say he sought to pitch his tent among the Danites in his mother's right and, being justly opposed, fell to strife — the mixed seed bringing forth the bitter fruit of blasphemy.

So the witnesses make the verse the occasion: The witnesses note the mixed parentage: an Egyptian father, an Israelite mother of Dan. The witnesses note the went out among Israel: dwelling among the people. The witnesses note the strove together: a quarrel breaking out in the camp. The witnesses note the evil manners, good laws: the sin occasioning the law that follows. The expositors observe the mixed parentage: an Egyptian father, an Israelite mother of Dan. The expositors observe the went out among Israel: dwelling among the people. The expositors observe the strove together: a quarrel breaking out in the camp. The expositors observe the evil manners, good laws: the sin occasioning the law that follows. The believer is taught to mark the mixed parentage, the went out among Israel, the strove together, the evil manners, good laws, and to honour the LORD in his worship. The verse thus records that a quarrel in the camp, sprung from the mixed seed, was the occasion of the law — evil manners begetting good laws.
11:
"And the Israelitish woman's son blasphemed the name of the LORD, and cursed. And they brought him unto Moses... and they put him in ward, that the mind of the LORD might be shewed them." The witnesses note the man, cast in the quarrel, breaking into blasphemy against the Name and cursing. The witnesses note that Gill notes he fell into outrageous blasphemies against God who made Israel's laws, and cursed the judges who sentenced him; the Jews held he pronounced the ineffable Name itself. The witnesses note that his mother is named as of the tribe of Dan, the sin set in its history. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the cast in the quarrel — defeated, he turned his rage upward; the blasphemed the Name — outrageous words against the LORD; the and cursed — cursing the judges who sentenced him; the against God's honour — the act striking at the honour of God himself.

The witnesses agree the blasphemy sprang from a heart in rebellion: defeated in his cause, the man turned his rage against God himself and his ordinances, daring to curse by the very Name of the LORD; the gravity of the act — striking at the honour of God — set it apart as a crime above the quarrel that occasioned it.

So the witnesses make the verse the blasphemy: The witnesses note the cast in the quarrel: defeated, he turned his rage upward. The witnesses note the blasphemed the Name: outrageous words against the LORD. The witnesses note the and cursed: cursing the judges who sentenced him. The witnesses note the against God's honour: the act striking at the honour of God himself. The expositors observe the cast in the quarrel: defeated, he turned his rage upward. The expositors observe the blasphemed the Name: outrageous words against the LORD. The expositors observe the and cursed: cursing the judges who sentenced him. The expositors observe the against God's honour: the act striking at the honour of God himself. The believer is taught to mark the cast in the quarrel, the blasphemed the Name, the and cursed, the against God's honour, and to honour the LORD in his worship. The verse thus records that the man, defeated, blasphemed the Name of the LORD and cursed — a crime striking at the honour of God, set above the quarrel that occasioned it.
12:
"And they put him in ward, that the mind of the LORD might be shewed them." The witnesses note the blasphemer kept in custody until the mind of the LORD should be made known concerning him. The witnesses note that Gill notes he was put in some known prison in the camp. The witnesses note that Ellicott observes the Mosaic law nowhere enacts imprisonment as a punishment; this was safe custody till his cause was judged by the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the put in ward — kept in custody in the camp; the not as punishment — imprisonment nowhere enacted as a penalty; the till the mind of the LORD — awaiting God's own judgment; the Moses waiting on God — seeking the divine mind before sentence.

The witnesses agree the pause was reverent, not uncertain: though blasphemy was plainly a breach of the third commandment, Moses would not act on his own judgment in so grave a case but waited on God, who answered from the mercy seat (Exod. 25:22) — a pattern of seeking the divine mind before pronouncing in matters of life and death.

So the witnesses make the verse the man in ward: The witnesses note the put in ward: kept in custody in the camp. The witnesses note the not as punishment: imprisonment nowhere enacted as a penalty. The witnesses note the till the mind of the LORD: awaiting God's own judgment. The witnesses note the Moses waiting on God: seeking the divine mind before sentence. The expositors observe the put in ward: kept in custody in the camp. The expositors observe the not as punishment: imprisonment nowhere enacted as a penalty. The expositors observe the till the mind of the LORD: awaiting God's own judgment. The expositors observe the Moses waiting on God: seeking the divine mind before sentence. The believer is taught to mark the put in ward, the not as punishment, the till the mind of the LORD, the Moses waiting on God, and to honour the LORD in his worship. The verse thus records that the blasphemer was held till the mind of the LORD was shown — Moses waiting on God before sentence, a pattern of seeking the divine mind in grave matters.
13:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note the LORD answering Moses' appeal concerning the blasphemer, speaking from the place of his presence. The witnesses note that Gill sets it that God spoke from off the mercy seat in the holy of holies, where he had promised to commune with Moses. The witnesses note that Ellicott judges it probable that Moses received the divine directions in the sanctuary, by Urim or otherwise. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD spake — god answering Moses' appeal; the from the mercy seat — speaking where he promised to commune; the not human counsel — the case too grave to decide alone; the a standing law — the sentence and the rule given together.

The witnesses agree the answer came from God himself, not from human counsel: the case of blasphemy was too grave for Moses to decide alone, and the LORD spoke from the mercy seat (Exod. 25:22) to lay down both the sentence on this man and a standing law — judgment in the weightiest matter resting on the express word of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the divine answer: The witnesses note the the LORD spake: God answering Moses' appeal. The witnesses note the from the mercy seat: speaking where he promised to commune. The witnesses note the not human counsel: the case too grave to decide alone. The witnesses note the a standing law: the sentence and the rule given together. The expositors observe the the LORD spake: God answering Moses' appeal. The expositors observe the from the mercy seat: speaking where he promised to commune. The expositors observe the not human counsel: the case too grave to decide alone. The expositors observe the a standing law: the sentence and the rule given together. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD spake, the from the mercy seat, the not human counsel, the a standing law, and to honour the LORD and his law. The verse thus records that the LORD answered Moses from the mercy seat — judgment in the weightiest matter resting on the express word of God, not human counsel.
14:
"Bring forth him that hath cursed without the camp; and let all that heard him lay their hands upon his head, and let all the congregation stone him." The witnesses note the sentence: the blasphemer brought outside the camp, the hearers laying hands on his head, the whole congregation stoning him. The witnesses note that Gill notes he was brought without the camp to show he had no part in Israel, the place of execution being outside as later without the city. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set vv.14-16, the hearers laying their hands on his head and all the congregation stoning him. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the without the camp — cast out, having no part in Israel; the the hearers lay hands — the witnesses putting the guilt on his head; the all the congregation — the whole people joining in the execution; the the honour of God — owned as the concern of all Israel.

The witnesses agree the rite was solemn and corporate: the witnesses laid their hands on his head, putting the guilt where it belonged and clearing themselves, and the whole congregation joined in the execution, owning the honour of God as the concern of all; the casting out beyond the camp marked the blasphemer as cut off from the people of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the sentence executed: The witnesses note the without the camp: cast out, having no part in Israel. The witnesses note the the hearers lay hands: the witnesses putting the guilt on his head. The witnesses note the all the congregation: the whole people joining in the execution. The witnesses note the the honour of God: owned as the concern of all Israel. The expositors observe the without the camp: cast out, having no part in Israel. The expositors observe the the hearers lay hands: the witnesses putting the guilt on his head. The expositors observe the all the congregation: the whole people joining in the execution. The expositors observe the the honour of God: owned as the concern of all Israel. The believer is taught to mark the without the camp, the the hearers lay hands, the all the congregation, the the honour of God, and to honour the LORD and his law. The verse thus records that the blasphemer was stoned outside the camp, the witnesses laying on hands and all the congregation joining — the honour of God owned as the concern of all.
15:
"And thou shalt speak unto the children of Israel, saying, Whosoever curseth his God shall bear his sin." The witnesses note the standing law now laid down: whoever curses his God shall bear his sin. The witnesses note that Clarke reads "curseth" as he who makes light of God, who treats him and sacred things without due reverence, and shall bear the guilt. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the LORD here lays down a general law beyond the particular offender. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a general law — beyond the one offender, a standing rule; the curseth his God — makes light of him without due reverence; the bear his sin — the guilt resting on the offender; the the honour of God — held sacred among his people.

The witnesses agree the case gave rise to a permanent statute: the irreverent contempt of God — making light of him and holy things — is no small fault but a sin whose guilt the offender must bear; the law fixes the principle that the honour of God is to be held sacred among his people, and its contempt answered.

So the witnesses make the verse the law of cursing God: The witnesses note the a general law: beyond the one offender, a standing rule. The witnesses note the curseth his God: makes light of him without due reverence. The witnesses note the bear his sin: the guilt resting on the offender. The witnesses note the the honour of God: held sacred among his people. The expositors observe the a general law: beyond the one offender, a standing rule. The expositors observe the curseth his God: makes light of him without due reverence. The expositors observe the bear his sin: the guilt resting on the offender. The expositors observe the the honour of God: held sacred among his people. The believer is taught to mark the a general law, the curseth his God, the bear his sin, the the honour of God, and to honour the LORD and his law. The verse thus records that whoever curses his God shall bear his sin — a standing law fixing that the honour of God is sacred and its contempt answered.
16:
"And he that blasphemeth the name of the LORD, he shall surely be put to death... as well the stranger, as he that is born in the land, when he blasphemeth the name of the LORD, shall be put to death." The witnesses note the heavier sentence on open blasphemy of the Name — death, and that for stranger and native alike. The witnesses note that Gill explains blasphemy is not the bare uttering of the Name but speaking ill and contemptuously of God in any of his names, perfections, or works. The witnesses note that Clarke reads the verb as "pierces" or "transfixes" the Name, the Name by which the divine Essence was especially marked. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the put to death — the heavier sentence for open blasphemy; the not the bare Name — but contemptuous speech of God himself; the pierces the Name — reviling the Name that marks the divine Essence; the stranger and native — all alike bound and answerable.

The witnesses agree the gravity of the penalty measured the gravity of the offence: to revile the very Name of God was the highest affront a creature could offer his Maker, and the law made no distinction of stranger or native — all alike were bound to honour God, and all alike answerable for blaspheming him, the equality itself a witness to the universal claim of God's honour.

So the witnesses make the verse blasphemy of the Name: The witnesses note the put to death: the heavier sentence for open blasphemy. The witnesses note the not the bare Name: but contemptuous speech of God himself. The witnesses note the pierces the Name: reviling the Name that marks the divine Essence. The witnesses note the stranger and native: all alike bound and answerable. The expositors observe the put to death: the heavier sentence for open blasphemy. The expositors observe the not the bare Name: but contemptuous speech of God himself. The expositors observe the pierces the Name: reviling the Name that marks the divine Essence. The expositors observe the stranger and native: all alike bound and answerable. The believer is taught to mark the put to death, the not the bare Name, the pierces the Name, the stranger and native, and to honour the LORD and his law. The verse thus records that the open blasphemer of the Name was to die, stranger and native alike — the equality a witness to the universal claim of God's honour.
17:
"And he that killeth any man shall surely be put to death." The witnesses note the law of life for life — he that kills a man shall surely die. The witnesses note that Gill corrects the narrowing of this to Israelites only; the original law (Gen. 9:6) respects any man whatever. The witnesses note that Clarke joins blasphemy and murder as the two crimes here punished with death — the one against God's honour, the other against his image. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the killeth any man — the murderer to be surely put to death; the any man whatever — the law reaching all, not Israel only; the beside blasphemy — murder set next the crime against God's honour; the God's image in man — the sanctity of life its ground.

The witnesses agree the placing of murder beside blasphemy is significant: to revile God and to destroy his image in man are the two great affronts to the Maker, and both alike forfeit life; God has absolutely required that life go for life, the sanctity of human life resting on its being made in his image.

So the witnesses make the verse life for life: The witnesses note the killeth any man: the murderer to be surely put to death. The witnesses note the any man whatever: the law reaching all, not Israel only. The witnesses note the beside blasphemy: murder set next the crime against God's honour. The witnesses note the God's image in man: the sanctity of life its ground. The expositors observe the killeth any man: the murderer to be surely put to death. The expositors observe the any man whatever: the law reaching all, not Israel only. The expositors observe the beside blasphemy: murder set next the crime against God's honour. The expositors observe the God's image in man: the sanctity of life its ground. The believer is taught to mark the killeth any man, the any man whatever, the beside blasphemy, the God's image in man, and to honour the LORD and his law. The verse thus records that the murderer was to die, life going for life — murder set beside blasphemy, the affront to God's image answered as the affront to his honour.
18:
"And he that killeth a beast shall make it good; beast for beast." The witnesses note the lesser case set beside the greater — he who kills another's beast must make it good, beast for beast. The witnesses note that Gill notes he must pay its value, or give another as good in its place. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set vv.18-21, the killing of a beast made good by restitution. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the killeth a beast — another's animal destroyed by him; the make it good — its value paid, or another as good given; the beast for beast — restitution, not death, required for the brute; the the gradation — man's life above the beast in justice's scale; the property upheld — the wrong to a neighbour's goods made good.

The witnesses agree the law marks the difference between man and beast in the scale of justice: the man's life is required for a man's, but for a beast only its worth, restitution and not death; the gradation itself teaches the dignity of human life above the brute, while still upholding the right of property and the duty of making good a wrong.

So the witnesses make the verse beast for beast: The witnesses note the killeth a beast: another's animal destroyed by him. The witnesses note the make it good: its value paid, or another as good given. The witnesses note the beast for beast: restitution, not death, required for the brute. The witnesses note the the gradation: man's life above the beast in justice's scale. The witnesses note the property upheld: the wrong to a neighbour's goods made good. The expositors observe the killeth a beast: another's animal destroyed by him. The expositors observe the make it good: its value paid, or another as good given. The expositors observe the beast for beast: restitution, not death, required for the brute. The expositors observe the the gradation: man's life above the beast in justice's scale. The expositors observe the property upheld: the wrong to a neighbour's goods made good. The believer is taught to mark the killeth a beast, the make it good, the beast for beast, the the gradation, the property upheld, and to honour the LORD and his law. The verse thus records that the killing of a beast was made good by restitution, not death — the gradation teaching the dignity of human life above the brute, while upholding the right of property.
19:
"And if a man cause a blemish in his neighbour; as he hath done, so shall it be done to him;" The witnesses note the law of retaliation for bodily injury — as a man has done to his neighbour, so shall it be done to him. The witnesses note that Gill reads it of any hurt, mutilation, or deformity caused by striking. The witnesses note that Calvin sees the necessity of the law: without it, the practised villain would break his brother's leg or arm and laugh at the poor man, at God, and at his Law. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the cause a blemish — any hurt or deformity inflicted on a neighbour; the as he hath done — the same measure returned upon him; the to restrain cruelty — that the villain not injure with impunity; the the magistrate's rule — justice, not private revenge.

The reformed reader (Calvin) gives the purpose: the law of retaliation, in use among other nations too, was enacted to restrain cruelty — that the strong, who might injure with impunity, should know the same measure awaited them; it is the magistrate's rule, not a warrant for private revenge, justice meeting the injury with an equal weight.

So the witnesses make the verse the law of injury: The witnesses note the cause a blemish: any hurt or deformity inflicted on a neighbour. The witnesses note the as he hath done: the same measure returned upon him. The witnesses note the to restrain cruelty: that the villain not injure with impunity. The witnesses note the the magistrate's rule: justice, not private revenge. The expositors observe the cause a blemish: any hurt or deformity inflicted on a neighbour. The expositors observe the as he hath done: the same measure returned upon him. The expositors observe the to restrain cruelty: that the villain not injure with impunity. The expositors observe the the magistrate's rule: justice, not private revenge. The believer is taught to mark the cause a blemish, the as he hath done, the to restrain cruelty, the the magistrate's rule, and to honour the LORD and his law. The verse thus records that the law of retaliation met the injury with an equal weight — a magistrate's rule to restrain cruelty, not a warrant for private revenge.
20:
"Breach for breach, eye for eye, tooth for tooth: as he hath caused a blemish in a man, so shall it be done to him again." The witnesses note the famous measure of the law of retaliation — breach for breach, eye for eye, tooth for tooth. The witnesses note that Gill records the Jewish reading, that this is not to be taken strictly or literally but for the price or value of the injury, paid in money. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set it as the jus talionis, the injury repaid in kind by the magistrate (cf. Exod. 21:23-25). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the breach for breach — the injury answered in kind; the the literal reading — exact retaliation by the magistrate; the the pecuniary reading — the price or value of the harm paid; the exact and equal justice — the principle on which both agree.

Here the witnesses divide on the manner of the law: the grammatical readers (Keil and Delitzsch), with the plain words, take it as literal retaliation administered by the judge; while Gill, with the Jewish tradition, holds it meant the monetary value of the harm — yet both agree the principle is exact and equal justice, the very law our Lord takes up and carries beyond revenge in the Sermon on the Mount (Matt. 5:38).

So the witnesses make the verse eye for eye: The witnesses note the breach for breach: the injury answered in kind. The witnesses note the the literal reading: exact retaliation by the magistrate. The witnesses note the the pecuniary reading: the price or value of the harm paid. The witnesses note the exact and equal justice: the principle on which both agree. The expositors observe the breach for breach: the injury answered in kind. The expositors observe the the literal reading: exact retaliation by the magistrate. The expositors observe the the pecuniary reading: the price or value of the harm paid. The expositors observe the exact and equal justice: the principle on which both agree. The believer is taught to mark the breach for breach, the the literal reading, the the pecuniary reading, the exact and equal justice, and to honour the LORD and his law. The verse thus records that the injury was met with an equal measure — whether literally or in its value, the principle exact and equal justice, the law Christ takes up in the Sermon on the Mount (Matt. 5:38).
21:
"And he that killeth a beast, he shall restore it: and he that killeth a man, he shall be put to death." The witnesses note the two cases set side by side again — restitution for a beast, death for a man. The witnesses note that Gill notes the repetition for confirmation, though Jarchi distinguishes this (of wounding a beast) from the former (of killing it outright). The witnesses note that Ellicott takes the verse as a repetition of the laws of vv.17-18. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the killeth a beast — restitution made for the animal; the killeth a man — death the only answer for a life; the the repetition — driving home the great distinction; the the scale of justice — the man beyond price, the beast a thing of worth.

The witnesses agree the repetition drives home the great distinction of the chapter: the worth of a beast is a thing of money, but the life of a man is beyond price and answered only with life; set together, the two cases fix the scale of justice and the surpassing value of the man made in God's image.

So the witnesses make the verse the two cases joined: The witnesses note the killeth a beast: restitution made for the animal. The witnesses note the killeth a man: death the only answer for a life. The witnesses note the the repetition: driving home the great distinction. The witnesses note the the scale of justice: the man beyond price, the beast a thing of worth. The expositors observe the killeth a beast: restitution made for the animal. The expositors observe the killeth a man: death the only answer for a life. The expositors observe the the repetition: driving home the great distinction. The expositors observe the the scale of justice: the man beyond price, the beast a thing of worth. The believer is taught to mark the killeth a beast, the killeth a man, the the repetition, the the scale of justice, and to honour the LORD and his law. The verse thus records that restitution answered for a beast and death for a man — the repetition fixing the scale of justice and the surpassing value of the man made in God's image.
22:
"Ye shall have one manner of law, as well for the stranger, as for one of your own country: for I am the LORD your God." The witnesses note the principle of equal law — one rule for stranger and native alike, grounded in the one LORD. The witnesses note that Gill applies it to all the foregoing — blasphemy, killing of man or beast, and bodily injury — one law for all. The witnesses note that Clarke praises equal laws, where each receives the same protection and privilege, as the boast of a sound political constitution. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the one manner of law — the same rule for every case; the stranger and native — no double standard of justice; the equal protection — each receiving the same right and shield; the I am the LORD — the impartial God the ground of equal law.

The witnesses agree the impartiality of the law reflects the impartiality of God: the stranger is not held to a lesser justice nor denied an equal protection, for the LORD is God of all alike; the same standard binds and shields every man — a principle the Wesleyan reader marks as the very glory of a just and well-ordered commonwealth.

So the witnesses make the verse one law for all: The witnesses note the one manner of law: the same rule for every case. The witnesses note the stranger and native: no double standard of justice. The witnesses note the equal protection: each receiving the same right and shield. The witnesses note the I am the LORD: the impartial God the ground of equal law. The expositors observe the one manner of law: the same rule for every case. The expositors observe the stranger and native: no double standard of justice. The expositors observe the equal protection: each receiving the same right and shield. The expositors observe the I am the LORD: the impartial God the ground of equal law. The believer is taught to mark the one manner of law, the stranger and native, the equal protection, the I am the LORD, and to honour the LORD and his law. The verse thus records that one law bound stranger and native alike — the impartiality of the law reflecting the impartiality of God, the glory of a just commonwealth.
23:
"And Moses spake to the children of Israel, that they should bring forth him that had cursed out of the camp, and stone him with stones. And the children of Israel did as the LORD commanded Moses." The witnesses note the close: Moses delivered the LORD's command, and Israel carried out the sentence on the blasphemer. The witnesses note that Gill notes they took the blasphemer, led him out of the camp, laid their hands on him, and stoned him till he died, as the Lord had commanded. The witnesses note that the people obeyed exactly the word God had given through Moses. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses spake — delivering the LORD's command to Israel; the brought forth and stoned — the blasphemer led out and executed; the did as commanded — the people obeying exactly; the God's honour vindicated — the deed sealing the gravity of blasphemy.

The witnesses agree the chapter ends in prompt and full obedience: the sentence the LORD had given was executed without delay or softening, Israel doing as he commanded; the narrative seals the gravity of blasphemy with the deed, and shows the people, instructed by God's own answer, vindicating his honour in the earth.

So the witnesses make the verse the sentence carried out: The witnesses note the Moses spake: delivering the LORD's command to Israel. The witnesses note the brought forth and stoned: the blasphemer led out and executed. The witnesses note the did as commanded: the people obeying exactly. The witnesses note the God's honour vindicated: the deed sealing the gravity of blasphemy. The expositors observe the Moses spake: delivering the LORD's command to Israel. The expositors observe the brought forth and stoned: the blasphemer led out and executed. The expositors observe the did as commanded: the people obeying exactly. The expositors observe the God's honour vindicated: the deed sealing the gravity of blasphemy. The believer is taught to mark the Moses spake, the brought forth and stoned, the did as commanded, the God's honour vindicated, and to honour the LORD and his law. The verse thus records that Israel carried out the sentence in full obedience — the deed sealing the gravity of blasphemy and vindicating the honour of God in the earth.
